title: Makeup on Cotton
stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (lipid-based). Foundation, concealer, and face makeup contain oil/silicone vehicle (dimethicone, cyclomethicone, mineral oil), pigment particles (iron oxide, titanium dioxide), waxes, and emulsifiers. The oil vehicle bonds to fabric through hydrophobic interaction while pigment particles embed physically. Requires dry-side treatment for the oil vehicle followed by wet-side for residual pigment. Cotton (cellulose fiber) tolerates most chemicals well.
whyThisWorks: Cosmetics contain oils, waxes, and pigments — the oil spreads the color deeper. Cotton absorbs liquids quickly and holds onto stains deep in the weave. NSD or a degreaser breaks down the oil so it becomes water-soluble and can be rinsed away.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply POG directly to the oil stain. POG is a volatile dry solvent with strong affinity for lipids — it dissolves oil droplets on contact. Tamp gently and blot with absorbent cloth.
2. If POG plateaus, apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. Blot with clean absorbent cloth between applications. Never use on acetate fabrics.
3. Apply NSD to emulsify any remaining oil micro-droplets and remove solvent residue. The detergent surrounds oil particles and allows them to be flushed away. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
4. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to restore fiber to its natural state. Blot dry and allow to air dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: warm for rinsing — warm water helps emulsify residual oil; treat with solvent first before introducing water
warnings: Rubbing instead of blotting — spreads the stain
Not testing in an inconspicuous area first
Using too aggressive a chemical without checking surface compatibility

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test + identify the makeup type
homeSolutions.instruction: Before you treat, check: is this foundation, lipstick, mascara, eyeshadow, or a waterproof/long-wear formula? Waterproof and long-wear makeup need rubbing alcohol in step 6, everything else does not. On an inside seam, dab a drop of rubbing alcohol if you'll use it — let sit 60 seconds and blot with a white cloth. If the cloth picks up dye, stop and hand-wash cold with Dawn only, then go to step 8.

homeSolutions.agent: The oil rule: don't add water first
homeSolutions.instruction: Most makeup is oil and pigment bound together. Water repels oil, so rinsing first just smears the stain into a bigger one. Instead: go dry → solvent → water, in that order. Also: do not put the garment in the dryer until the stain is fully out. Heat polymerizes the oil vehicle into the cotton fiber and locks the pigment in.

homeSolutions.agent: Lift any solid or caked makeup
homeSolutions.instruction: For a chunk of lipstick, a smear of waxy foundation, or dried mascara: lift it off with the edge of a spoon or a plastic card, scraping away from the center of the stain. Don't rub — you're removing what's sitting on the surface so the next step can reach the fiber. Use a new section of a white paper towel to dab up anything that transfers.

homeSolutions.agent: Absorbent powder — pull the oil out
homeSolutions.instruction: Sprinkle cornstarch, baking soda, or unscented talc generously over the stain. Let it sit 10–15 minutes. The powder wicks the oil out of the fiber before it sets. Brush off with a dry white cloth or a soft toothbrush, then vacuum or shake off the residue. You may see the powder yellow or tint — that's the oil leaving. If so, apply a second round of fresh powder and wait again.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — work it in, dry
homeSolutions.instruction: Put a small drop of clear Dawn dish soap directly on the stain — no water yet. Work it into the fiber with a fingertip or the bristles of a soft toothbrush in gentle circles. Let it sit 5–10 minutes. Dawn is specifically designed to lift oil (it's what wildlife rescuers use on oil-soaked birds). Blot with a clean damp cloth from the back of the fabric.

homeSolutions.agent: Rubbing alcohol spot treatment — waterproof/long-wear only
homeSolutions.instruction: If the makeup is waterproof mascara, long-wear foundation, lip stain, or waxy lipstick, those formulas are built to resist water. Dab a small amount of isopropyl alcohol (70% or 91%) on a white cloth and press onto the stain. Let it sit 2–3 minutes, then blot. The alcohol dissolves the wax and silicone components Dawn leaves behind. Skip this step on non-waterproof makeup — it's not needed.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Test on your step-1 patch first. Alcohol can dissolve some dyes. Never apply to silk, acetate, or rayon — only cotton.

homeSolutions.instruction: Launder the garment on a warm cycle (not hot) with your normal detergent. If a shadow remains when it's done, DO NOT put it in the dryer — heat will set what's left permanently. Instead hang to air-dry, inspect in daylight, and repeat steps 5–6 before the next wash. Only dry on heat when the stain is completely invisible.

If a color shadow is still there after a full wash — common with red lipstick, orange foundation, or deep-toned eyeshadow — pretreat with an enzyme stain remover (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Zout) per the label. Let it dwell 30 minutes. Then launder cold. Enzymes target the protein binders in many long-wear formulas that Dawn can't reach.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a dry cleaner for structured or delicate pieces
homeSolutions.instruction: For tailored cotton (a blazer, structured dress) or if the stain survived two home rounds, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Makeup on cotton — type: [foundation/lipstick/mascara/waterproof], treated at home with Dawn and [alcohol/enzyme]. No heat applied. Please treat dry-side first for oil, then wet-side for residual pigment." That tells them the exact protocol order and saves them diagnostic time.

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain. Wait 5 minutes.
2. Brush off the powder with a clean white cloth.
3. Put a drop of Dawn dish soap on the stain. Blot it in.
4. Let it sit for 2–3 minutes. Rinse with cold water.
5. Repeat if needed. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Try a dry cleaning solvent (Carbona Stain Devil #5 or Goo Gone). If the stain survives two rounds, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents and specialized equipment.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fabric.
Never add water to an oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, and you'll spread it.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone.

neverDo: Apply heat before the oil is removed — polymerizes into the fiber
Add water first — doesn't dissolve oil and spreads pigment
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates most agents including bleach on whites. For colored cotton, test peroxide first. High heat can set pigment permanently. OxiClean safe on cotton.

escalation.when: Oil component removes but pigment particles remain embedded in the cotton weave
escalation.whatToTell: Makeup/foundation stain on cotton, oil and pigment combination. Treated at home with NSD. Please use dry-side solvent protocol followed by wet-side.

contextQuestions: Is this foundation, concealer, mascara, or eyeshadow?
Is the stain fresh or has it been set?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
Is the cotton white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Mascara has a wax and carbon black pigment component — may need extra solvent time. Waterproof formulas are more resistant to water-based cleaning. Dryer exposure bakes pigment into the fiber. On colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam first.
